64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b3d.question: One poem titled for this place repeats, “When I leave you I turn to stone / and when I come back I turn to stone”. The speaker of another poem set in this place claims, “I don’t walk, I fly, I become another, / transfigured” after describing their hands as “two doves / on the (+) cross hovering and carrying the earth”. This place titles a poem whose speaker states, “I walk from one epoch to another without a memory / to guide me”. In this place, the speaker responds to a “woman (*) soldier” by saying, “You killed me… and I forgot, / like you, to die”. For 10 points, name this city that titles a poem set “within the ancient walls” by Mahmoud Darwish.
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b3d.question_sanitized: One poem titled for this place repeats, "When I leave you I turn to stone / and when I come back I turn to stone". The speaker of another poem set in this place claims, "I don't walk, I fly, I become another, / transfigured" after describing their hands as "two doves / on the (+) cross hovering and carrying the earth". This place titles a poem whose speaker states, "I walk from one epoch to another without a memory / to guide me". In this place, the speaker responds to a "woman (*) soldier" by saying, "You killed me... and I forgot, / like you, to die". For 10 points, name this city that titles a poem set "within the ancient walls" by Mahmoud Darwish.
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b3d.answer: Jerusalem [or al-Quds; accept “In Jerusalem”] (The first poem is “Jerusalem” by the unrelated Najwan Darwish)
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b3d.answer_sanitized: Jerusalem [or al-Quds; accept "In Jerusalem"] (The first poem is "Jerusalem" by the unrelated Najwan Darwish)

64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b44.question: This collection includes poems beginning “for you beautiful ones my thought / is not changeable” and “their heart grew cold / they let their wings down”. The speaker of another poem in this collection says, “I beg you / do not break with hard pains” and describes “what I want to happen most of all / in my crazy (+) heart”. A poem in this collection describes a man who “opposite you / sits and listens close / to your sweet speaking”. (*) This collection includes poems titled for a “Deathless” figure of “the spangled mind” and one often titled phainetai moi. A line from Fragment 22 titles, for 10 points, what collection of Sappho translations by Anne Carson?
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b44.question_sanitized: This collection includes poems beginning "for you beautiful ones my thought / is not changeable" and "their heart grew cold / they let their wings down". The speaker of another poem in this collection says, "I beg you / do not break with hard pains" and describes "what I want to happen most of all / in my crazy (+) heart". A poem in this collection describes a man who "opposite you / sits and listens close / to your sweet speaking". (*) This collection includes poems titled for a "Deathless" figure of "the spangled mind" and one often titled phainetai moi. A line from Fragment 22 titles, for 10 points, what collection of Sappho translations by Anne Carson?
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b44.answer: If Not, Winter: Fragments of Sappho [prompt on any answer referring to Sappho’s original work with “What book is that specific translation from?”]
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b44.answer_sanitized: If Not, Winter: Fragments of Sappho [prompt on any answer referring to Sappho's original work with "What book is that specific translation from?"]

64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b46.question: In a love poem by this man, he describes the “malice of thy tenderness” and states, “Dearest, my lips wax all too wise”. This man described himself as “Framed to defy the poison-dart” in that poem, in which he compares himself to (+) Mithridates. Another poem by this author states, “Highhearted youth comes not again” and describes a “Grey way whose violet signals are / The trysting and the twining star”. That poem by this author exclaims, “Ah star of (*) evil! star of pain!” and begins, “The eyes that mock me sign the way”. For 10 points, name this Irish poet who included “Bahnhofstrasse” in his collection Pomes Penyeach.
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b46.question_sanitized: In a love poem by this man, he describes the "malice of thy tenderness" and states, "Dearest, my lips wax all too wise". This man described himself as "Framed to defy the poison-dart" in that poem, in which he compares himself to (+) Mithridates. Another poem by this author states, "Highhearted youth comes not again" and describes a "Grey way whose violet signals are / The trysting and the twining star". That poem by this author exclaims, "Ah star of (*) evil! star of pain!" and begins, "The eyes that mock me sign the way". For 10 points, name this Irish poet who included "Bahnhofstrasse" in his collection Pomes Penyeach.
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b46.answer: James Joyce [or James Augustine Aloysius Joyce] (The first poem is “Though I Thy Mithridates Were”)
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b46.answer_sanitized: James Joyce [or James Augustine Aloysius Joyce] (The first poem is "Though I Thy Mithridates Were")

64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b4f.question: One of this author’s poems describes “angular / starched consonants” that “braid the tongue at its root” and “foreign cities where the sun's a ghost”. That poem by this author ends by stating, “and once more on our lips we'll taste Greek (+) salt”. Another poem by this author claims that “in silences / things yield and almost betray / their ultimate secrets”, and describes a “little path” that goes through “cane-tufts” and the title (*) plants. This poet wrote, “Laureate poets / seem to wander among plants / no one knows” in a poem from his collection Cuttlefish Bones. For 10 points, name this Italian poet of “The Lemon Trees”.
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b4f.question_sanitized: One of this author's poems describes "angular / starched consonants" that "braid the tongue at its root" and "foreign cities where the sun's a ghost". That poem by this author ends by stating, "and once more on our lips we'll taste Greek (+) salt". Another poem by this author claims that "in silences / things yield and almost betray / their ultimate secrets", and describes a "little path" that goes through "cane-tufts" and the title (*) plants. This poet wrote, "Laureate poets / seem to wander among plants / no one knows" in a poem from his collection Cuttlefish Bones. For 10 points, name this Italian poet of "The Lemon Trees".

64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b53.question: This poet wrote, “If you could look away, / The pedestals of your feet might move” in a poem titled for a figure “Whose young have rooted / In the very flesh of her scalp”. This poet of “Medusa” wrote, “they do [a certain action] annually, or weekly, / Some even do it daily” in a poem that describes (+) “peanut eaters, entertained”. In that poem, this poet references a February 11, 1963 event by describing “the ability / To imagine the body, head in (*) oven, / Orphaning children”. For 10 points, name this British-Australian poet of “My Mother”, the daughter of Sylvia Plath.
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b53.question_sanitized: This poet wrote, "If you could look away, / The pedestals of your feet might move" in a poem titled for a figure "Whose young have rooted / In the very flesh of her scalp". In that poem, this poet references a February 11, 1963 event by describing "the ability / To imagine the body, head in (*) oven, / Orphaning children". For 10 points, name this British-Australian poet of "My Mother", the daughter of Sylvia Plath.
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b53.answer: Frieda Hughes [or Frieda Rebecca Hughes; prompt on Hughes; prompt on descriptions of the daughter of Sylvia Plath or Ted Hughes before mention]
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b53.answer_sanitized: Frieda Hughes [or Frieda Rebecca Hughes; prompt on Hughes; prompt on descriptions of the daughter of Sylvia Plath or Ted Hughes before mention]

64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b54.question: Two answers required. In a 2007 movie about the other, one of these two poets is portrayed by Ben Whishaw and is interrogated while smoking. One of these two poets claimed that “the bells went off” and “[i]t made perfect sense” upon reading the other’s quote (+) “I is another”. A relationship that one of these two poets had with another, older man is referenced by the statement, “Relationships have all been bad…But there’s no way I can compare” in the other’s (*) song, “You’re Gonna Make Me Lonesome When You Go”. For 10 points, name these two poets whose collections include Illuminations and Tarantula.

For 10 points, name these two poets whose collections include Illuminations and Tarantula.
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b54.answer: Arthur Rimbaud and Bob Dylan [or Jean Nicolas Arthur Rimbaud and Robert Allen Zimmerman] (The first line refers to the 2007 film I'm Not There)

64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b5c.question: The speaker of one poem inspired by this work repeats “God bless you! God forgive you!” and claims, “I have been considerate and good, / I have been bent and broken”. That Richard Price poem composed of lines from this work is titled for the line “I am greatly changed”. Another poem inspired by this work states, (+)“Spinster. I stink and remember” and ends, “Don’t think it’s only the heart that b-b-b-breaks”. That poem inspired by this work opens, “Beloved sweetheart bastard”, and describes “Love’s hate behind a white veil” and “the (*) dress / yellowing”. For 10 points, name this novel that inspired Carol Ann Duffy’s poem “Havisham”.

64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b5c.answer: Great Expectations [accept the 59th or final chapter of Great Expectations]

64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b63.question: This poem’s speaker describes a concept that “will splash against the eye, sink into lymph, like some Aeolian nymph”. Like an older work, this poem’s final section switches to quatrains with an AABB rhyme scheme and opens, “Apollo, fling your garland down”. This poem claims, “each grave is the limit of the earth” and asks, “Where are you, (+) Magi, you who read men’s eyes?”. This poem describes a “front door flinched in frost” after stating, “He died at start of year, in January”. For 15 points, name this three-part poem that implores, “Thomas Stearns, don't dread the sheep, / or the reaper's deadly sweep”, by Joseph Brodsky.
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b63.question_sanitized: This poem's speaker describes a concept that "will splash against the eye, sink into lymph, like some Aeolian nymph". Like an older work, this poem's final section switches to quatrains with an AABB rhyme scheme and opens, "Apollo, fling your garland down". This poem claims, "each grave is the limit of the earth" and asks, "Where are you, (+) Magi, you who read men's eyes?". This poem describes a "front door flinched in frost" after stating, "He died at start of year, in January". For 15 points, name this three-part poem that implores, "Thomas Stearns, don't dread the sheep, / or the reaper's deadly sweep", by Joseph Brodsky.
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b63.answer: “Verses on the Death of T. S. Eliot”
64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b63.answer_sanitized: "Verses on the Death of T. S. Eliot"

64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b65.question: This poet ended one poem by describing figures “of equal size” who “shout, dream, weep, sing”. That poem by this poet opens by describing “Shadows that only I can see”, and contrasts the title characters’ cries of (+) “I tire!” and “I am dying!”. In another poem, this poet described a figure with “eyes of glass”, who “coils on a stick” and “can move without feet” before instructing, “Hit him with an ax and he dies”. This poet repeatedly exclaimed (*) “Mayombe—bombe—mayombé” in a “Chant for Killing a Snake”. For 10 points, name this Cuban poet of “Ballad of the Two Grandfathers” and “Sensemayá”.

64cef7255ca7dc5dd99e2b65.answer_sanitized: Nicolas Guillen [or Nicolas Cristobal Guillen Batista]
